verses.text: These are the nations which the LORD left, to prove Israel by them, even as many of Israel as had not known all the wars of Canaan; only that the generations of the children of Israel might know, to teach them war, at the least such as before knew nothing thereof: namely, five lords of the Philistines, and all the Canaanites, and the Sidonians, and the Hivites that dwelt in mount Lebanon, from mount Baal-hermon unto the entering in of Hamath. They were to prove Israel by them, to know whether they would hearken unto the commandments of the LORD, which he commanded their fathers by the hand of Moses. Guidance: God's twofold purpose for the leftover nations: prove (test loyalty) and teach (train for war). The wilderness generation knew Pharaoh's chariots; their grandchildren had only heard the stories. God will not raise a soft people in a hard world. The same Philistines who menace Israel for centuries are also Israel's curriculum. The discomfort is the lesson; the test is the teacher.

The children of Israel dwelt among the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites: and they took their daughters to be their wives, and gave their daughters to their sons, and served their gods. The children of Israel did evil in the sight of the LORD, and forgat the LORD their God, and served Baalim and the groves. Guidance: Three escalating verbs: dwelt, married, served. Living next door to Canaanites became marrying Canaanites became worshipping with Canaanites. Joshua 23:12 warned this exactly. 'Forgat the LORD their God' — not couldn't remember, but stopped trying. The 'groves' (asherim) — Asherah's wooden poles — appear in private yards. Once neighbors share gods, the LORD's name leaves the dinner table.

Therefore the anger of the LORD was hot against Israel, and he sold them into the hand of Chushan-rishathaim king of Mesopotamia: and the children of Israel served Chushan-rishathaim eight years. When the children of Israel cried unto the LORD, the LORD raised up a deliverer to the children of Israel, who delivered them, even Othniel son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother. Guidance: The cycle of chapter 2 plays out in detail. Chushan-rishathaim's name means 'Cushan of double wickedness' — Israel gets exactly what its idolatry asked for, a doubly wicked oppressor. Eight years of servitude, then a cry, then a deliverer. Othniel is no random figure — he is the giant-slayer of Hebron (Josh 15) and Caleb's son-in-law. The first judge is a man whose faith resume is already established. God does not waste prepared people.

Eight years of servitude, then a cry, then a deliverer. Othniel is no random figure — he is the giant-slayer of Hebron (Josh 15) and Caleb's son-in-law. The first judge is a man whose faith resume is already established. God does not waste prepared people.

The Spirit of the LORD came upon him, and he judged Israel, and went out to war: and the LORD delivered Chushan-rishathaim king of Mesopotamia into his hand; and his hand prevailed against Chushan-rishathaim. The land had rest forty years. And Othniel son of Kenaz died. Guidance: 'The Spirit of the LORD came upon him' — the first judge gets the chapter's purest verdict. Spirit, war, victory, rest. Forty years of peace from one Spirit-filled man. The phrase 'the Spirit of the LORD' will return on Gideon (6:34), Jephthah (11:29), and Samson (13:25, 14:6, 14:19, 15:14) — but never again paired this cleanly with quiet judging and lasting rest. Othniel is the gold standard.

The children of Israel did evil again in the sight of the LORD: and the LORD strengthened Eglon the king of Moab against Israel, because they had done evil in the sight of the LORD. He gathered unto him the children of Ammon and Amalek, and went and smote Israel, and possessed the city of palm trees. So the children of Israel served Eglon the king of Moab eighteen years. Guidance: 'The LORD strengthened Eglon' — God Himself empowers the enemy as the discipline. Eglon recruits Ammon (Israel's distant cousin via Lot) and Amalek (their oldest tribal enemy from Exod 17). The 'city of palm trees' is Jericho — the very city God's hand had toppled with trumpets is now Moab's outpost. Eighteen years of paying tribute on the ground where God had once parted the river. The reversal is dramatic; Israel feels every year of it.

When the children of Israel cried unto the LORD, the LORD raised them up a deliverer, Ehud son of Gera, a Benjamite, a man lefthanded: and by him the children of Israel sent a present unto Eglon the king of Moab. Ehud made him a dagger which had two edges, of a cubit length; and he did gird it under his raiment upon his right thigh. He brought the present unto Eglon king of Moab: and Eglon was a very fat man. Guidance: Ehud is a 'lefthanded Benjamite' — the tribal name means 'son of the right hand,' so the joke is intentional. His handicap (literally 'bound on the right hand') is his secret weapon. Guards routinely searched the left thigh for weapons; the right was assumed clean. The dagger is short (a cubit, about 18 inches) and double-edged. The narrator does not hide Eglon's heaviness because it will matter for the assassination. God uses bodies and disabilities not as obstacles but as instruments.

When he had made an end to offer the present, he sent away the people that bare the present. But he himself turned again from the quarries that were by Gilgal, and said, I have a secret errand unto thee, O king: who said, Keep silence. And all that stood by him went out from him. Ehud came unto him; and he was sitting in a summer parlour, which he had for himself alone. Ehud said, I have a message from God unto thee. He arose out of his seat. Ehud put forth his left hand, took the dagger from his right thigh, thrust it into his belly: and the haft also went in after the blade; and the fat closed upon the blade, so that he could not draw the dagger out of his belly; and the dirt came out. Then Ehud went forth through the porch, and shut the doors of the parlour upon him, and locked them. Guidance: A dark comedy in tight prose. 'I have a secret errand' lures the guards out; 'I have a message from God' lures the king up. The king stands — perhaps in reverence to receive what he thinks is a divine word — and gets a blade instead of a syllable. The grotesque detail ('the fat closed upon the blade, the dirt came out') is intentional; the narrator wants the reader to taste the absurd downfall of a tyrant. Eglon was Israel's idol of fear; the verse drains it of dignity. Ehud, cool as ice, locks the door behind him.

When he was gone out, his servants came; and when they saw that, behold, the doors of the parlour were locked, they said, Surely he covereth his feet in his summer chamber. They tarried till they were ashamed: and, behold, he opened not the doors of the parlour; therefore they took a key, and opened them: and, behold, their lord was fallen down dead on the earth. Ehud escaped while they tarried, and passed beyond the quarries, and escaped unto Seirath. Guidance: The servants think the king is using the bathroom — 'covering his feet' is a Hebrew euphemism. They wait until they are embarrassed at the waiting. The bawdy timing is the writer's gift to Ehud; every minute the servants squirm outside, Ehud puts distance between himself and Moab. By the time the door is unlocked, he is across the border. Even palace bureaucracy works for the LORD when He is delivering His people.

When he was come, that he blew a trumpet in the mountain of Ephraim, and the children of Israel went down with him from the mount, and he before them. He said unto them, Follow after me: for the LORD hath delivered your enemies the Moabites into your hand. They went down after him, and took the fords of Jordan toward Moab, and suffered not a man to pass over. They slew of Moab at that time about ten thousand men, all lusty, and all men of valour; and there escaped not a man. So Moab was subdued that day under the hand of Israel. The land had rest fourscore years. Guidance: A single trumpet from a man with blood still on his hands gathers a nation. 'The LORD hath delivered' — Ehud gives God the credit before the militia ever swings. Taking the Jordan fords is brilliant tactic: any Moabite trying to flee home is trapped between Israel and the river. Ten thousand fall; eighty years of peace follow. The longest period of rest in Judges came from the most lopsided deliverer — a left-handed man with a short dagger and a great God.

After him was Shamgar son of Anath, which slew of the Philistines six hundred men with an ox goad: and he also delivered Israel. Guidance: A single-verse judge. Shamgar's name is non-Israelite (probably Hurrian); 'son of Anath' may suggest a Canaanite goddess in his family tree. An ox goad — a long stick with one iron point for prodding, the other a small chisel for cleaning the plough — is a farmer's tool, not a weapon. Six hundred Philistines with what was in his hand. The whole chapter has been a study in unlikely instruments: Othniel's family, Ehud's wrong hand, Shamgar's farming stick. The deliverer is not the weapon. The LORD is the weapon; the deliverer is just the open hand.
